claude_vision.referral_reason: Decreased hearing and cerumen impaction (wax in canal). Patient is being referred to ENT for wax removal and evaluation of decreased hearing, with a hearing test required prior to the ENT physician visit.
relevant_clinical_findings: Chief complaint: Decreased hearing (bilateral sensorineural hearing loss, ICD-389.10 / H91.90)
Cerumen impaction noted on physical exam: 'dec hearing, WAX IN CANAL. L canal is nl' — wax noted, left canal normal
Patient self-reports hearing problem: 'Do you have a hearing problem now? Yes'
ENT review of systems: 'Complains of decreased hearing and nasal congestion'
Referring provider note: 'HE C/O OF DR HEARING HE C/O OF DEC VISION, HE WOULD LIKE A REFERRAL TO ENT AND OPTHAL'
Problem #8 in impression: 'Cerumen impaction (ICD10-H61.20) — will refer to ENT for wax removal and dec hearing'
Scheduling note: 'Ok to schedule a new patient visit with Steven Hasday, MD or Conor McLaughlin, MD. Hearing test needed first.'
missing_information: Audiogram or formal hearing test results — none attached; referral notes hearing test is required before ENT visit but no prior audiometry is included
Onset timeline for hearing loss is not documented — unclear whether this is acute/sudden or chronic/gradual, which is critical for urgency triage
No insurance authorization number included in the referral packet
Referral form does not include patient date of birth or full patient name (redacted/illegible in available documents)
No prior ENT evaluation records attached

provider_urgency_label.label: none found
provider_urgency_label.source: The Hill Physicians Referral/Consultation form does not contain a marked urgency or priority field. The ROMG office visit note and scheduling comments also contain no urgency designation. No terms such as 'URGENT,' 'STAT,' 'ROUTINE,' or 'Priority' were identified anywhere in the document set.

evidence: Hearing loss is described as bilateral sensorineural hearing loss (chronic diagnosis ICD-389.10) appearing on a long-standing problem list — no documentation of sudden or acute onset
Physical exam states 'dec hearing, WAX IN CANAL' — consistent with gradual/chronic hearing loss and cerumen impaction, not sudden sensorineural hearing loss
Scheduling note requires hearing test first, suggesting this is a workup-stage referral, not an emergency
No airway compromise, no foreign body, no abscess, no malignancy, no nasal fracture, no tongue tie, and no peritonsillar abscess mentioned anywhere in the referral
criteria_check.provider_label: No urgency label found in document
criteria_check.reasoning: No urgent ENT criteria are matched — the hearing loss appears chronic/gradual rather than sudden-onset, cerumen impaction is non-emergent, and the referring provider applied no urgency designation; therefore this is a routine referral appropriate for the standard queue.

claude_vision.next_steps: Standard scheduling applies; however, per the referring provider's explicit instruction, the patient must complete a hearing test (audiogram) first — schedule the hearing test and book the ENT new patient visit with Dr. Steven Hasday or Dr. Conor McLaughlin approximately 15 minutes after. Note: if at any point prior to scheduling the patient reports sudden or acute-onset hearing loss, escalate immediately for priority review within 1 week per sudden hearing loss criteria.
claude_vision.summary: This is a routine ENT referral for an adult male patient with bilateral sensorineural hearing loss and cerumen impaction, referred by Dr. Charanjit Sandhu of River Oaks Medical Group. The referring provider requests that a hearing test be completed before the new patient ENT visit with Dr. Hasday or Dr. McLaughlin at 1111 Exposition Blvd, Suite 700, Sacramento. No urgent ENT criteria are met, no imaging is involved, and no urgency label was applied by the referring provider; this referral belongs in the standard scheduling queue with the prerequisite audiogram coordinated first.
